meta.name: IAT Internet Addiction Test
meta.description: The IAT is a 20-item questionnaire measuring the extent of internet use on daily life, social interactions, work, and emotions. It is the most widely used tool in internet addiction research.
meta.author: Young (1998)
meta.reference: Young KS. Internet addiction: The emergence of a new clinical disorder. CyberPsychology & Behavior. 1998;1(3):237-244.
meta.instruction: Please rate how often each statement applies to you.

questions.text: How often do you find that you stay online longer than you intended
How often do you neglect household chores to spend more time online
How often do you prefer the excitement of the internet to intimacy with your partner
How often do you form new relationships with fellow online users
How often do others in your life complain to you about the amount of time you spend online
How often do your grades or school work suffer because of the amount of time you spend online
How often do you check your email/social media before something else that you need to do
How often does your job performance or productivity suffer because of the internet
How often do you become defensive or secretive when anyone asks you what you do online
How often do you block out disturbing thoughts about your life with soothing thoughts of the internet
How often do you find yourself anticipating when you will go online again
How often do you fear that life without the internet would be boring, empty, and joyless
How often do you snap, yell, or act annoyed if someone bothers you while you are online
How often do you lose sleep due to late-night log-ins
How often do you feel preoccupied with the internet when offline, or fantasize about being online
How often do you find yourself saying 'just a few more minutes' when online
How often do you try to cut down the amount of time you spend online and fail
How often do you try to hide how long you've been online
How often do you choose to spend more time online over going out with others
How often do you feel depressed, moody, or nervous when you are offline, which goes away once you are back online

ranges.label: Normal
ranges.description: Your internet usage is within the normal range. No significant signs of internet addiction detected.
suggestions: Balance your online and offline activities
Maintain a rich social life and diverse hobbies

ranges.label: Mild Internet Addiction
ranges.description: Your internet usage may be starting to affect your daily life. Consider monitoring your online time and habits.
suggestions: Set daily time limits for internet use and stick to them
Develop leisure activities that don't rely on the internet
Pay attention to the impact of internet use on your sleep and work
If you find it hard to control, consider consulting a professional

ranges.label: Moderate Internet Addiction
ranges.description: Internet use is causing noticeable problems in your daily life, social interactions, or work performance.
suggestions: Consider seeking professional help from a counselor
Create a specific plan to reduce internet time with family support
Increase outdoor activities and face-to-face social interactions
Identify emotional triggers or situations that lead to excessive use
Consider using screen time management tools

ranges.label: Severe Internet Addiction
ranges.description: Internet use is severely impacting your quality of life, relationships, and daily functioning. Please seek professional help immediately.
suggestions: Strongly recommended to consult a psychiatrist or psychotherapist immediately
Structured treatment programs (e.g., cognitive behavioral therapy) may be needed
Inform family and friends about your difficulties and seek support
Gradually reduce internet time rather than quitting abruptly
Address possible underlying emotional issues (e.g., depression, anxiety)
